The Backpropagation algorithm for a math student

Saeed Damadi1, Golnaz Moharrer2, Mostafa Cham2, Jinglai Shen1
Department of Mathematics and Statistics1superscriptDepartment of Mathematics and Statistics1{}^{1}\text{Department of Mathematics and Statistics}, Department of Information Systems2superscriptDepartment of Information Systems2{}^{2}\text{Department of Information Systems}
University of Maryland, Baltimore County (UMBC)
Baltimore, MD 21250
sdamadi1, golnazm1, mcham2, shenj@umbc.edu
Abstract

A Deep Neural Network (DNN) is a composite function of vector-valued functions, and in order to train a DNN, it is necessary to calculate the gradient of the loss function with respect to all parameters. This calculation can be a non-trivial task because the loss function of a DNN is a composition of several nonlinear functions, each with numerous parameters. The Backpropagation (BP) algorithm leverages the composite structure of the DNN to efficiently compute the gradient. As a result, the number of layers in the network does not significantly impact the complexity of the calculation. The objective of this paper is to express the gradient of the loss function in terms of a matrix multiplication using the Jacobian operator. This can be achieved by considering the total derivative of each layer with respect to its parameters and expressing it as a Jacobian matrix. The gradient can then be represented as the matrix product of these Jacobian matrices. This approach is valid because the chain rule can be applied to a composition of vector-valued functions, and the use of Jacobian matrices allows for the incorporation of multiple inputs and outputs. By providing concise mathematical justifications, the results can be made understandable and useful to a broad audience from various disciplines.

1 Introduction

Understanding the process of training a (Deep) Neural Networks (D)NNs, as illustrated in Fig 1, is not straightforward because it involves many detailed parts. Additionally, modern and sophisticated DNNs are often provided as pre-trained models in Python packages such as Pytorch Paszke et al. (2019) and TensorFlow Abadi et al. (2016), which can make it difficult for users to fully understand the training process. These packages abstract away many of the implementation details, making it more accessible for users to use these models for their own tasks, but also making it less transparent for the user to understand the inner workings of the model.

These pre-trained off-the-shelf models can solve variety of tasks such as computer vision or language processing. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are currently the most widely used architecture for image classification and other computer vision tasks. Some examples of successful CNN architectures for image classification include ResNet He et al. (2016), Inception Szegedy et al. (2015), DenseNet Huang et al. (2017), and EfficientNet Tan & Le (2019). CNNs models are capable of solving image segmentation and object detection which can be done using U-Net Ronneberger et al. (2015) and YOLO Redmon et al. (2016). For natural language processing tasks, transformer models like BERT Devlin et al. (2018), GPT-3 Brown et al. (2020), and T5 Raffel et al. (2020) have achieved state-of-the-art performance on many benchmarks.

Fig 1 shows the training process that utilizes the Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) algorithm Robbins & Monro (1951) to minimize the loss function of a DNN. As its name suggests, the SGD algorithm requires calculating the gradient111Please refer Def. 2 in Appendix for the definition of the gradient of a scalar-valued function. of the loss function of a DNN. All different variants of the SGD algorithm require calculating at least a single gradient associated with a single sample, i.e., 𝐱𝐱\mathbf{x} in Fig. 1. This calculation is a non-trivial task because the loss function of a DNN is a composition of several nonlinear vector-valued functions where each one has numerous parameters. The Backpropagation (BP) algorithm, introduced by Rumelhart et al. (1986b) is an efficient way to calculate the gradient of the loss function of a DNN. This algorithm leverages the composite structure of a DNN to efficiently calculate the gradient of the loss function with respect to the model’s parameters, i.e., 𝜽𝜽\bm{\theta} in Fig. 1.

In this paper we are going to calculate the gradient of the loss function of a DNN associated with a single sample. The gradient will be derived as the matrix multiplication of Jacobian matrices 222Please refer Def. 1 in Appendix for the definition of a Jacobian matrix of a vector-valued function.. The derivation will be done by considering the total derivative of each layer with respect to its parameters and expressing it as a Jacobian matrix. The gradient can then be represented as the matrix product of these Jacobian matrices. This approach is well-founded because the chain rule is valid for the Jacobian operator. Hence, the Jacobian operator can be applied to a composition of vector-valued functions. We provide concise mathematical justifications so the results can be made understandable and useful to a broad audience from various disciplines, even those without a deep understanding of the mathematics involved. This is particularly important when communicating complex technical concepts to non-experts, as it allows for a clear and accurate understanding of the results. Additionally, using mathematical notation allows for precise and unambiguous statements of results, which can facilitate replication and further research in the field.

Our results is summarized in Alg. 1 for L𝐿L number of layers. As Alg. 1 shows the matrix multiplication is done iteratively. The iterative nature comes from the fact that the loss function of a DNN is defined as a composition of L+1𝐿1L+1 functions where the last one ℓℓ\ell is the final function which measures the loss (error) of the prediction and the actual value, i.e., ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^)ℓ𝐲^𝐲\ell(\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}) in Fig 1 where 𝐲^^𝐲\hat{\mathbf{y}} is the prediction and 𝐲𝐲\mathbf{y} the actual value.

The algorithm presented in Alg. 1 is explained and justified by calculating the gradient of the loss function for networks with one, two, and three layers. These calculations provide insights into the gradients of loss functions for generic neural networks and demonstrate how the gradient of a single-layer network can serve as a model for the last layer of any DNN. Additionally, the calculation of a two-layer network is used to extend the calculation beyond two-layer networks, as seen in the calculation of the gradient of the loss function for LeNet-100-300 LeCun et al. (1998), which is a three-layer network. Finally, we show how convolutional layers can be converted to linear layers in order to calculate their Jacobian matrices. These results can be used to calculate the gradient of the loss function of a CNN.

2 Notation

The letters x𝑥x, 𝐱𝐱\mathbf{x}, and 𝐖𝐖\mathbf{W} denote a scalar, vector, and matrix, respectively. The letter 𝐈𝐈\mathbf{I} represents the identity matrix. The i𝑖i-th element of a vector 𝐱𝐱\mathbf{x} is denoted by xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i}. Likewise, wi​jsubscript𝑤𝑖𝑗w_{ij} denotes the i​j𝑖𝑗ij-th element of a matrix 𝐖𝐖\mathbf{W} located at the i𝑖i-th row and j𝑗j-th column (sometimes written as wi,jsubscript𝑤𝑖𝑗w_{i,j} for clarity). Also, 𝐖i∙\mathbf{W}{i\bullet} and 𝐖∙j∙𝐖𝑗\mathbf{W}{\bullet j} denote the i𝑖i-th row and the j𝑗j-th column of the matrix, respectively. The vector form of a matrix 𝐖𝐖\mathbf{W} is denoted by Vec​(𝐖)Vec𝐖\text{Vec}(\mathbf{W}), where each column of 𝐖𝐖\mathbf{W} is stacked on top of each other, with the first column at the top. The letter 𝐟𝐟\mathbf{f} is reserved for a vector-valued non-linear activation function of a layer in a DNN (NN), where 𝐳𝐳\mathbf{z} and 𝐚𝐚\mathbf{a} are its input and output, respectively, i.e., 𝐚=𝐟​(𝐳)𝐚𝐟𝐳\mathbf{a}=\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{z}). The letter 𝐱𝐱\mathbf{x} is reserved for the input to a DNN (NN), and y𝑦y or 𝐲𝐲\mathbf{y} are reserved for the scalar or vector label of the input 𝐱𝐱\mathbf{x}. The predictions of a DNN (NN) associated with y𝑦y or 𝐲𝐲\mathbf{y} are denoted by y^^𝑦\hat{y} or 𝐲^^𝐲\hat{\mathbf{y}}, respectively. Superscripted index inside square brackets denotes the layer of a DNN (NN), e.g., 𝐳[l]superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝑙\mathbf{z}^{[l]} is the 𝐳𝐳\mathbf{z} vector corresponding to the l𝑙l-th layer.

3 Result

As we have explained earlier, the goal of this paper is to take the first step towards training a DNN, which involves calculating the gradient of a loss function with respect to all parameters of the DNN, i.e., ∇𝜽ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝜽))subscript∇𝜽ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝜽\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\ell(\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\bm{\theta})), where 𝜽𝜽\bm{\theta} is the vector of parameters, 𝐲^^𝐲\hat{\mathbf{y}} is the predicted value by the network, 𝐲𝐲\mathbf{y} is the true value, and ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝜽))ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝜽\ell(\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\bm{\theta})) is the loss incurred to predict the output. To achieve this goal, two important observations can make the task easier. First, it involves separating the last-layer activation function from the network. Second, it involves using the relationship between the Jacobian operator and the gradient.

To fulfill the first step, we observe the following equality:

ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝜽))=ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟[L]​(𝐳[L]​(𝜽)))ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝜽ℓ𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}=\ell\Big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}^{[L]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}\Big{)}

which is a consequence of the fact that 𝐲^=𝐟[L]​(𝐳[L]​(𝜽))^𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽\hat{\mathbf{y}}=\mathbf{f}^{[L]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}, where 𝐟[L]superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿\mathbf{f}^{[L]} is the last activation layer and 𝐳[L]​(𝜽)superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta}) is its corresponding input. This equality can be illustrated more clearly as shown in Fig. 2.

Second observation utilizes the relationship between the Jacobian operator and the gradient of a scalar-valued function stated in the following lemma.

Lemma 1 (Jacobian and gradient of a scalar-valued function).

For f:ℝn→ℝ:𝑓→superscriptℝ𝑛ℝf:\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R} as a scalar-valued differentiable function ∇𝐱f​(𝐱)=(𝐉𝐱​f​(𝐱))⊤subscript∇𝐱𝑓𝐱superscriptsubscript𝐉𝐱𝑓𝐱top\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}f(\mathbf{x})=\Big{(}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{x}}f(\mathbf{x})\Big{)}^{\top} where ∇𝐱f​(𝐱)subscript∇𝐱𝑓𝐱\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}f(\mathbf{x}) is the gradient and 𝐉𝐱​f​(𝐱)subscript𝐉𝐱𝑓𝐱\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{x}}f(\mathbf{x}) is the Jacobian matrix of f𝑓f at point 𝐱∈ℝn𝐱superscriptℝ𝑛\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n} respectively.

Proof.

The equality follows from the definitions of a Jacobian matrix as defined in Def. 1 and the gradient of a scalar-valued function as defined in Def. in 2 in Appendix. ∎

By using the second observation as ∇𝐱f​(𝐱)=(𝐉𝐱​f​(𝐱))⊤,subscript∇𝐱𝑓𝐱superscriptsubscript𝐉𝐱𝑓𝐱top\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}f(\mathbf{x})=\Big{(}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{x}}f(\mathbf{x})\Big{)}^{\top}, and making use of the first one as

ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝜽))=ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟[L]​(𝐳[L]​(𝜽))),ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝜽ℓ𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}=\ell\Big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}^{[L]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}\Big{)},

one can write the following:

∇𝜽ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝜽))subscript∇𝜽ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝜽\displaystyle\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\bm{\theta})\big{)} =(𝐉𝜽​ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝜽)))⊤absentsuperscriptsubscript𝐉𝜽ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝜽top\displaystyle=\Big{(}\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}\Big{)}^{\top} (1)
=(𝐉𝜽​ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟[L]​(𝐳[L]​(𝜽))))⊤absentsuperscriptsubscript𝐉𝜽ℓ𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽top\displaystyle=\Bigg{(}\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\ell\Big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}^{[L]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}\Big{)}\Bigg{)}^{\top}
=(𝐉𝐳[L]​ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟[L]​(𝐳[L]))​𝐉𝜽​𝐳[L]​(𝜽))⊤absentsuperscriptsubscript𝐉superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿ℓ𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿subscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽top\displaystyle=\Bigg{(}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}^{[L]}}\ell\Big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}^{[L]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}\big{)}\Big{)}\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta})\Bigg{)}^{\top}
=(𝐉𝜽​𝐳[L]​(𝜽))⊤​(𝐉𝐳[L]​ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟[L]​(𝐳[L])))⊤absentsuperscriptsubscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽topsuperscriptsubscript𝐉superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿ℓ𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿top\displaystyle=\Bigg{(}\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta})\Bigg{)}^{\top}\Bigg{(}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}^{[L]}}\ell\Big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}^{[L]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}\big{)}\Big{)}\Bigg{)}^{\top}
=(𝐉𝜽​𝐳[L]​(𝜽))⊤​∇𝐳[L]ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟[L]​(𝐳[L]))absentsuperscriptsubscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽topsubscript∇superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿ℓ𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿\displaystyle=\Bigg{(}\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta})\Bigg{)}^{\top}\nabla_{\mathbf{z}^{[L]}}\ell\Big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}^{[L]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}\big{)}\Big{)}

The advantage of Equation (1) is that it separates the original gradient calculation into two separate calculations, i.e., 𝐉𝜽​𝐳[L]​(𝜽)subscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta}) and ∇𝐳[L]ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟[L]​(𝐳[L]))subscript∇superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿ℓ𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿\nabla_{\mathbf{z}^{[L]}}\ell\Big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}^{[L]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}\big{)}\Big{)}.

The calculation of the second term is straightforward because the choice of a loss function and the last activation function in a DNN are not arbitrary. This is illustrated in Fig. 3, which shows three common combinations, each associated with a different problem.

In a binary classification problem, a sigmoid function is used as the last activation function together with Binary Cross Entropy (BCE) loss, as defined in Definition 3 and Definition 5 in the Appendix, respectively. Similarly, in a classification problem with more than two classes, a softmax function is used as the last activation function together with Cross Entropy (CE) loss, as defined in Definition 4 and Definition 6 in the Appendix, respectively. In a regression problem, where the goal is to predict a continuous value, the last-layer activation function is an identity function, and Square Error (SE) is used as the loss function, as defined in Definition 7. This means that the predicted output 𝐲^^𝐲\hat{\mathbf{y}} is equal to the final layer’s output 𝐳[L]superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿\mathbf{z}^{[L]}. Table 1 shows the combination of the last activation function with its corresponding loss and the expression for ∇𝐳[L]ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟[L]​(𝐳[L]))subscript∇superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿ℓ𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿\nabla_{\mathbf{z}^{[L]}}\ell\Big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}^{[L]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}\big{)}\Big{)}. For the derivation, please refer to Appendix D.

More work is required to calculate the second term, i.e., 𝐉𝜽​𝐳[L]​(𝜽)subscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta}). In the following subsections, we show how to derive this calculation concisely for any number of layers.

3.1 Gradient of a one-layer network

We will now focus on computing 𝐉𝜽​𝐳[L]​(𝜽)subscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta}). This calculation can be facilitated by starting with a single layer neural network that is capable of solving a classification problem. The single layer network plays a crucial role in gradient computation as it can be considered as the last layer of any deep neural network (DNN). Due to the presence of only one layer, the superscripts in 𝐳[L]superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿\mathbf{z}^{[L]} and 𝐟[L]superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝐿\mathbf{f}^{[L]} can be omitted, giving us 𝐉𝜽​𝐳​(𝜽):=𝐉𝜽​𝐳[L]​(𝜽)assignsubscript𝐉𝜽𝐳𝜽subscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿𝜽\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}(\bm{\theta}):=\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[L]}(\bm{\theta}). The network depicted in Fig. 4 is designed to perform three-class classification based on inputs with four features. Consequently, the weight matrix 𝐖𝐖\mathbf{W} is a 4×3434\times 3 and the bias vector 𝐛𝐛\mathbf{b} is a 3×1313\times 1, i.e., 𝐖∈ℝ4×3𝐖superscriptℝ43\mathbf{W}\in\mathbb{R}^{4\times 3} and 𝐛∈ℝ3×1𝐛superscriptℝ31\mathbf{b}\in\mathbb{R}^{3\times 1}. As shown in the concise representation of the network in the bottom part of Fig. 4, 𝐳∈ℝ3𝐳superscriptℝ3\mathbf{z}\in\mathbb{R}^{3}, i.e., 𝐳=(𝐖)⊤​𝐱+𝐛𝐳superscript𝐖top𝐱𝐛\mathbf{z}=\big{(}\mathbf{W}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}. This vector 𝐳𝐳\mathbf{z} is a vector-valued function including 151515 parameters where these parameters are all elements in 𝐖𝐖\mathbf{W} and 𝐛𝐛\mathbf{b}, i.e., 15=4×3+3×115433115=4\times 3+3\times 1. According to the definition of Jacobian matrix as defined in Def. 1 in Appendix, 𝐉𝐖,𝐛​(𝐳​(𝐖,𝐛))subscript𝐉𝐖𝐛𝐳𝐖𝐛\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W},\mathbf{b}}\big{(}\mathbf{z}(\mathbf{W},\mathbf{b})\big{)} is a 3×153153\times 15 matrix, i.e., 𝐉𝐖,𝐛​(𝐳​(𝐖,𝐛))∈ℝ3×ℝ15subscript𝐉𝐖𝐛𝐳𝐖𝐛superscriptℝ3superscriptℝ15\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W},\mathbf{b}}\big{(}\mathbf{z}(\mathbf{W},\mathbf{b})\big{)}\in\mathbb{R}^{3}\times\mathbb{R}^{15} .

For notational simplicity, all the parameters are denoted by 𝜽𝜽\bm{\theta} which is a vector in ℝ15superscriptℝ15\mathbb{R}^{15}, and is constructed by the process of vectorization. The vectorization process stacks each column of 𝐖𝐖\mathbf{W} on top of each other, with the first column on the top to create Vec​(𝐖)Vec𝐖\text{Vec}(\mathbf{W}). Then, the vector of network parameters can be written as 𝜽⊤:=[(Vec​(𝐖))⊤(𝐛)⊤]⊤assignsuperscript𝜽topsuperscriptmatrixsuperscriptVec𝐖topsuperscript𝐛toptop\bm{\theta}^{\top}:=\begin{bmatrix}\big{(}\text{Vec}(\mathbf{W})\big{)}^{\top}&(\mathbf{b})^{\top}\end{bmatrix}^{\top}. Therefore to calculate 𝐉𝜽​𝐳​(𝜽)subscript𝐉𝜽𝐳𝜽\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}(\bm{\theta}) one can write the following:

𝐉𝜽​𝐳​(𝜽)subscript𝐉𝜽𝐳𝜽\displaystyle\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}(\bm{\theta}) =𝐉𝐖,𝐛​((𝐖)⊤​𝐱+𝐛)absentsubscript𝐉𝐖𝐛superscript𝐖top𝐱𝐛\displaystyle=\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W},\mathbf{b}}\Big{(}\big{(}\mathbf{W}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}\Big{)}
=𝐉𝐖,𝐛​([(𝐖∙1)⊤​𝐱+b1(𝐖∙2)⊤​𝐱+b2(𝐖∙3)⊤​𝐱+b3])absentsubscript𝐉𝐖𝐛matrixsuperscriptsubscript𝐖∙absent1top𝐱subscript𝑏1superscriptsubscript𝐖∙absent2top𝐱subscript𝑏2superscriptsubscript𝐖∙absent3top𝐱subscript𝑏3\displaystyle=\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W},\mathbf{b}}\Big{(}\begin{bmatrix}\big{(}\mathbf{W}_{\bullet 1}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+b_{1}\\ \big{(}\mathbf{W}_{\bullet 2}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+b_{2}\\ \big{(}\mathbf{W}_{\bullet 3}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+b_{3}\end{bmatrix}\Big{)}
=[𝐱⊤001000𝐱⊤001000𝐱⊤001]absentmatrixsuperscript𝐱top001000superscript𝐱top001000superscript𝐱top001\displaystyle=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}^{\top}&0&0&1&0&0\\ 0&\mathbf{x}^{\top}&0&0&1&0\\ 0&0&\mathbf{x}^{\top}&0&0&1\end{bmatrix}
=[𝐱⊤000𝐱⊤0𝐈3×300𝐱⊤]∈ℝ3×18absentmatrixsuperscript𝐱top00missing-subexpression0superscript𝐱top0subscript𝐈3300superscript𝐱topmissing-subexpressionsuperscriptℝ318\displaystyle=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}^{\top}&0&0&\\ 0&\mathbf{x}^{\top}&0&\mathbf{I}_{3\times 3}\\ 0&0&\mathbf{x}^{\top}&\end{bmatrix}\in\mathbb{R}^{3\times 18}

where 𝐖∙isubscript𝐖∙absent𝑖\mathbf{W}_{\bullet i} is a column of 𝐖𝐖\mathbf{W} for i=1,2,3𝑖123i=1,2,3 and 𝐈3×3subscript𝐈33\mathbf{I}_{3\times 3} appears because the derivative of each element of 𝐳𝐳\mathbf{z} with respect to components of 𝐛𝐛\mathbf{b} are either zero or one. Therefore the gradient of the loss function is calculated as follows

∇𝜽ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝜽))subscript∇𝜽ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝜽\displaystyle\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\bm{\theta})\big{)} =(𝐉𝜽​𝐳​(𝜽))⊤​∇𝐳ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟​(𝐳))absentsuperscriptsubscript𝐉𝜽𝐳𝜽topsubscript∇𝐳ℓ𝐲𝐟𝐳\displaystyle=\big{(}\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}^{\top}\nabla_{\mathbf{z}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{z})\big{)}
=−[𝐱000𝐱000𝐱𝐈3×3]​(𝐲−𝐲^)absentmatrix𝐱000𝐱000𝐱missing-subexpressionsubscript𝐈33missing-subexpression𝐲^𝐲\displaystyle=-\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}&0&0\\ 0&\mathbf{x}&0\\ 0&0&\mathbf{x}\\ &\mathbf{I}_{3\times 3}&\end{bmatrix}(\mathbf{y}-\hat{\mathbf{y}})

where the value for ∇𝐳ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟​(𝐳))subscript∇𝐳ℓ𝐲𝐟𝐳\nabla_{\mathbf{z}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{z})\big{)} is obtained from Tab. 1. The above gradient is similar to the gradient of one-layer networks whose weights are vectors not matrices as shown in Tab. 2. As it can be seen from Tab. 2 famous problems such as simple/multiple linear regression, simple binary classification, and logistic regression can be written as a one-layer network whose weight are vectors not matrices.

Although a one-layer network provides valuable understanding of the relationship between the Jacobian and the gradient of the loss function with respect to 𝐳𝐳\mathbf{z}, it is not practical in terms of performance, i.e., accuracy in prediction. To improve performance, adding more layers is recommended. As a result, the following subsection will demonstrate the calculation of the gradient of a two-layer network.

3.2 Gradient of a two-layer network

Studying a two-layer network can not only improve performance (accuracy) but also aid in developing a method for calculating the gradient of any deep neural network (DNN) with multiple layers. To demonstrate this, a two-layer network will be considered, as shown in Fig. 5 where block-wise model helps calculating the gradient of its loss.

Similar to a one-layer network, one can write ∇𝐳[2]ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟[2]​(𝐳[2]))=−(𝐲−𝐲^)subscript∇superscript𝐳delimited-[]2ℓ𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]2superscript𝐳delimited-[]2𝐲^𝐲\nabla_{\mathbf{z}^{[2]}}\ell\Big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}^{[2]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}\big{)}\Big{)}=-(\mathbf{y}-\hat{\mathbf{y}}) from Tab. 1. To calculate 𝐉𝜽​𝐳[2]​(𝜽)subscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]2𝜽\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}(\bm{\theta}), observe that it can be separated into parameters of second and first layer as does 𝜽𝜽\bm{\theta}. The separation for 𝜽𝜽\bm{\theta} can be written as the following:

𝜽⊤:=[(Vec​(𝐖[1]))⊤(𝐛[1])⊤(Vec​(𝐖[2]))⊤(𝐛[2])⊤]⊤assignsuperscript𝜽topsuperscriptmatrixsuperscriptVecsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]1topsuperscriptsuperscript𝐛delimited-[]1topsuperscriptVecsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2topsuperscriptsuperscript𝐛delimited-[]2toptop\bm{\theta}^{\top}:=\begin{bmatrix}\big{(}\text{Vec}(\mathbf{W}^{[1]})\big{)}^{\top}&(\mathbf{b}^{[1]})^{\top}&\big{(}\text{Vec}(\mathbf{W}^{[2]})\big{)}^{\top}&(\mathbf{b}^{[2]})^{\top}\end{bmatrix}^{\top}

which is used to write the below separation of Jacobian matrices:

𝐉𝜽​𝐳[2]​(𝜽)=[𝐉𝐖[1],𝐛[1]​(𝐳[2]​(𝜽))𝐉𝐖[2],𝐛[2]​(𝐳[2]​(𝜽))].subscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]2𝜽absentmatrixsubscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]2𝜽subscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]2superscript𝐛delimited-[]2superscript𝐳delimited-[]2𝜽\begin{aligned} \mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}(\bm{\theta})&=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[1]},\mathbf{b}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)}&\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[2]},\mathbf{b}^{[2]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)}\end{bmatrix}\end{aligned}.

Calculating the second term is straight forward because one can write the following:

𝐉𝐖[2],𝐛[2]​(𝐳[2]​(𝜽))subscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]2superscript𝐛delimited-[]2superscript𝐳delimited-[]2𝜽\displaystyle\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[2]},\mathbf{b}^{[2]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)} =𝐉𝐖[2],𝐛[2]​((𝐖[2])⊤​𝐚[1]+𝐛[2])absentsubscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]2superscript𝐛delimited-[]2superscriptsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2topsuperscript𝐚delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]2\displaystyle=\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[2]},\mathbf{b}^{[2]}}\Big{(}\big{(}\mathbf{W}^{[2]}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{a}^{[1]}+\mathbf{b}^{[2]}\Big{)}
=[(𝐚[1])⊤000(𝐚[1])⊤0𝐈3×300(𝐚[1])⊤]absentmatrixsuperscriptsuperscript𝐚delimited-[]1top00missing-subexpression0superscriptsuperscript𝐚delimited-[]1top0subscript𝐈3300superscriptsuperscript𝐚delimited-[]1topmissing-subexpression\displaystyle=\begin{bmatrix}\big{(}\mathbf{a}^{[1]}\big{)}^{\top}&0&0&\\ 0&\big{(}\mathbf{a}^{[1]}\big{)}^{\top}&0&\mathbf{I}_{3\times 3}\\ 0&0&\big{(}\mathbf{a}^{[1]}\big{)}^{\top}&\end{bmatrix}

where 𝐚[1]∈ℝ5superscript𝐚delimited-[]1superscriptℝ5\mathbf{a}^{[1]}\in\mathbb{R}^{5} because 𝐖[2]∈ℝ5×3superscript𝐖delimited-[]2superscriptℝ53\mathbf{W}^{[2]}\in\mathbb{R}^{5\times 3}. To calculate the second term first observe that 𝐖[2]superscript𝐖delimited-[]2\mathbf{W}^{[2]} is not a function of 𝐖[1]superscript𝐖delimited-[]1\mathbf{W}^{[1]} nor 𝐛[1]superscript𝐛delimited-[]1\mathbf{b}^{[1]}. Then one can write the following:

𝐉𝐖[1],𝐛[1]​(𝐳[2]​(𝜽))subscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]2𝜽\displaystyle\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[1]},\mathbf{b}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)} =𝐉𝐖[1],𝐛[1]​((𝐖[2])⊤​𝐚[1]+𝐛[2])absentsubscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]1superscriptsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2topsuperscript𝐚delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]2\displaystyle=\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[1]},\mathbf{b}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\big{(}\mathbf{W}^{[2]}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{a}^{[1]}+\mathbf{b}^{[2]}\Big{)}
=(𝐖[2])⊤​𝐉𝐖[1],𝐛[1]​(𝐚[1])absentsuperscriptsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2topsubscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]1superscript𝐚delimited-[]1\displaystyle=\big{(}\mathbf{W}^{[2]}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[1]},\mathbf{b}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{a}^{[1]}\Big{)}
=(𝐖[2])⊤​𝐉𝐖[1],𝐛[1]​(𝐟[1]​(𝐳[1]))absentsuperscriptsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2topsubscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]1superscript𝐟delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]1\displaystyle=\big{(}\mathbf{W}^{[2]}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[1]},\mathbf{b}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{f}^{[1]}(\mathbf{z}^{[1]})\Big{)}
=(𝐖[2])⊤​𝐉𝐖[1],𝐛[1]​(𝐟[1]​(𝐳[1]))absentsuperscriptsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2topsubscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]1superscript𝐟delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]1\displaystyle=\big{(}\mathbf{W}^{[2]}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[1]},\mathbf{b}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{f}^{[1]}(\mathbf{z}^{[1]})\Big{)}

The last equality is where the chain rule needs to be used in order to obtain an expression in terms of 𝐖[1]superscript𝐖delimited-[]1\mathbf{W}^{[1]} and 𝐛[1]superscript𝐛delimited-[]1\mathbf{b}^{[1]}, as shown below:

𝐉𝐖[1],𝐛[1]​(𝐳[2]​(𝜽))=(𝐖[2])⊤​𝐉𝐳[1]​(𝐟[1]​(𝐳[1]))​𝐉𝐖[1],𝐛[1]​((𝐖[1])⊤​𝐱+𝐛[1])=(𝐖[2])⊤​𝐉𝐳[1]​(𝐟[1]​(𝐳[1]))​[𝐱⊤000⋱0I00𝐱⊤].subscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]2𝜽absentsuperscriptsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2topsubscript𝐉superscript𝐳delimited-[]1superscript𝐟delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]1subscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]1superscriptsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]1top𝐱superscript𝐛delimited-[]1missing-subexpressionabsentsuperscriptsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2topsubscript𝐉superscript𝐳delimited-[]1superscript𝐟delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]1matrixsuperscript𝐱top000⋱0𝐼00superscript𝐱topmissing-subexpression\begin{aligned} \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[1]},\mathbf{b}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)}&=\big{(}\mathbf{W}^{[2]}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{f}^{[1]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[1]}\big{)}\Big{)}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[1]},\mathbf{b}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\big{(}\mathbf{W}^{[1]}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}^{[1]}\Big{)}\\ &=\big{(}\mathbf{W}^{[2]}\big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{f}^{[1]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[1]}\big{)}\Big{)}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}^{\top}&0&0\\ 0&\ddots&0&I\\ 0&0&\mathbf{x}^{\top}&\end{bmatrix}\end{aligned}.

Finally, the gradient would be the following:

∇𝜽ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝜽))=(𝐉𝜽​𝐳[2]​(𝜽))⊤​∇𝐳[2]ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟​(𝐳[2]))=−[[𝐱000⋱000𝐱𝐈5×5]​(𝐉𝐳[1]​(𝐟[1]​(𝐳[1])))⊤​𝐖[2][𝐚[1]000𝐚[1]000𝐚[1]𝐈3×3]]​(𝐲−𝐲^).subscript∇𝜽ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝜽absentsuperscriptsubscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]2𝜽topsubscript∇superscript𝐳delimited-[]2ℓ𝐲𝐟superscript𝐳delimited-[]2missing-subexpressionabsentmatrixmatrix𝐱000⋱000𝐱missing-subexpressionsubscript𝐈55missing-subexpressionsuperscriptsubscript𝐉superscript𝐳delimited-[]1superscript𝐟delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]1topsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2matrixsuperscript𝐚delimited-[]1000superscript𝐚delimited-[]1000superscript𝐚delimited-[]1missing-subexpressionsubscript𝐈33missing-subexpression𝐲^𝐲\begin{aligned} \nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}&=\big{(}\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}^{\top}\nabla_{\mathbf{z}^{[2]}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{z}^{[2]})\big{)}\\ &=-\begin{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}&0&0\\ 0&\ddots&0\\ 0&0&\mathbf{x}\\ &\mathbf{I}_{5\times 5}&\end{bmatrix}\Big{(}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{f}^{[1]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[1]}\big{)}\Big{)}\Big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{W}^{[2]}\\ \begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{a}^{[1]}&0&0\\ 0&\mathbf{a}^{[1]}&0\\ 0&0&\mathbf{a}^{[1]}\\ &\mathbf{I}_{3\times 3}&\end{bmatrix}\end{bmatrix}(\mathbf{y}-\hat{\mathbf{y}})\end{aligned}.

The next subsection will demonstrate the calculation of the gradient for a three-layer network, thereby illustrating the extension of a two-layer network gradient to an arbitrary number of layers.

3.3 Gradient of a three-layer network

In this subsection, we evaluate the gradient of a loss function for a LeNet-100-300-10 network architecture, which consists of 100, 300, and 10 units (neurons) in the first, second and third layer respectively LeCun et al. (1998). This network is used for image classification tasks LeCun (1998). The input to the network is a vectorized representation of 28×28282828\times 28 digit images (784784784 elements) and the output is a vector in ℝ10superscriptℝ10\mathbb{R}^{10}. The input to the LeNet-100-300-10 is a vector 𝐱∈ℝ784𝐱superscriptℝ784\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{784} representing the vectorized image. Similar to a one- and two-layer networks ∇𝐳[3]ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟[3]​(𝐳[3]))=−(𝐲−𝐲^)∈ℝ10subscript∇superscript𝐳delimited-[]3ℓ𝐲superscript𝐟delimited-[]3superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝐲^𝐲superscriptℝ10\nabla_{\mathbf{z}^{[3]}}\ell\Big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}^{[3]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[3]}\big{)}\Big{)}=-(\mathbf{y}-\hat{\mathbf{y}})\in\mathbb{R}^{10} which is obtained from Tab. 1. To calculate 𝐉𝜽​𝐳[3]​(𝜽)subscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝜽\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[3]}(\bm{\theta}), three Jacobian are needed to separate out the parameters of the layers as the following:

𝐉𝜽​𝐳[3]​(𝜽)=[𝐉𝐖[1],𝐛[1]​(𝐳[3]​(𝜽))𝐉𝐖[2],𝐛[2]​(𝐳[3]​(𝜽))𝐉𝐖[3],𝐛[3]​(𝐳[3]​(𝜽))].subscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝜽absentmatrixsubscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝜽subscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]2superscript𝐛delimited-[]2superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝜽subscript𝐉superscript𝐖delimited-[]3superscript𝐛delimited-[]3superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝜽\begin{aligned} \mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[3]}(\bm{\theta})&=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[1]},\mathbf{b}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[3]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)}&\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[2]},\mathbf{b}^{[2]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[3]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)}&\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{W}^{[3]},\mathbf{b}^{[3]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[3]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)}\end{bmatrix}\end{aligned}.

By following the same steps as for the two-layer network, the gradient can be calculated as:

∇𝜽ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝜽))=(𝐉𝜽​𝐳[3]​(𝜽))⊤​∇𝐳[3]ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟​(𝐳[3]))subscript∇𝜽ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝜽superscriptsubscript𝐉𝜽superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝜽topsubscript∇superscript𝐳delimited-[]3ℓ𝐲𝐟superscript𝐳delimited-[]3\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}=\big{(}\mathbf{J}_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbf{z}^{[3]}(\bm{\theta})\big{)}^{\top}\nabla_{\mathbf{z}^{[3]}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{z}^{[3]})\big{)}

where Jacobian matrices of layers 𝐉𝐖[1],𝐛[1]⊤​(𝐳[3]​(𝜽))subscriptsuperscript𝐉topsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]1superscript𝐛delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝜽\mathbf{J}^{\top}_{\mathbf{W}^{[1]},\mathbf{b}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[3]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)} , 𝐉𝐖[2],𝐛[2]⊤​(𝐳[3]​(𝜽))subscriptsuperscript𝐉topsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2superscript𝐛delimited-[]2superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝜽\mathbf{J}^{\top}_{\mathbf{W}^{[2]},\mathbf{b}^{[2]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[3]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)} , 𝐉𝐖[3],𝐛[3]⊤​(𝐳[3]​(𝜽))subscriptsuperscript𝐉topsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]3superscript𝐛delimited-[]3superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝜽\mathbf{J}^{\top}_{\mathbf{W}^{[3]},\mathbf{b}^{[3]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[3]}(\bm{\theta})\Big{)} are:

[𝐱000⋱000𝐱𝐈300×300]​(𝐉𝐳[1]​(𝐟[1]​(𝐳[1])))⊤​𝐖[2]​(𝐉𝐳[2]​(𝐟[2]​(𝐳[2])))⊤​𝐖[3],matrix𝐱000⋱000𝐱missing-subexpressionsubscript𝐈300300missing-subexpressionsuperscriptsubscript𝐉superscript𝐳delimited-[]1superscript𝐟delimited-[]1superscript𝐳delimited-[]1topsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]2superscriptsubscript𝐉superscript𝐳delimited-[]2superscript𝐟delimited-[]2superscript𝐳delimited-[]2topsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]3\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}&0&0\\ 0&\ddots&0\\ 0&0&\mathbf{x}\\ &\mathbf{I}_{300\times 300}&\end{bmatrix}\Big{(}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}^{[1]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{f}^{[1]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[1]}\big{)}\Big{)}\Big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{W}^{[2]}\Big{(}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}^{[2]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{f}^{[2]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}\big{)}\Big{)}\Big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{W}^{[3]},
[𝐚[1]000⋱000𝐚[1]𝐈100×100]​(𝐉𝐳[2]​(𝐟[2]​(𝐳[2])))⊤​𝐖[3],matrixsuperscript𝐚delimited-[]1000⋱000superscript𝐚delimited-[]1missing-subexpressionsubscript𝐈100100missing-subexpressionsuperscriptsubscript𝐉superscript𝐳delimited-[]2superscript𝐟delimited-[]2superscript𝐳delimited-[]2topsuperscript𝐖delimited-[]3\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{a}^{[1]}&0&0\\ 0&\ddots&0\\ 0&0&\mathbf{a}^{[1]}\\ &\mathbf{I}_{100\times 100}&\end{bmatrix}\Big{(}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}^{[2]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{f}^{[2]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[2]}\big{)}\Big{)}\Big{)}^{\top}\mathbf{W}^{[3]},
[𝐚[2]000⋱000𝐚[2]𝐈10×10],matrixsuperscript𝐚delimited-[]2000⋱000superscript𝐚delimited-[]2missing-subexpressionsubscript𝐈1010missing-subexpression\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{a}^{[2]}&0&0\\ 0&\ddots&0\\ 0&0&\mathbf{a}^{[2]}\\ &\mathbf{I}_{10\times 10}&\end{bmatrix},

and ∇𝐳[3]ℓ​(𝐲,𝐟​(𝐳[3]))=−(𝐲−𝐲^)subscript∇superscript𝐳delimited-[]3ℓ𝐲𝐟superscript𝐳delimited-[]3𝐲^𝐲\nabla_{\mathbf{z}^{[3]}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{z}^{[3]})\big{)}=-(\mathbf{y}-\hat{\mathbf{y}}) which is consistent with Alg. 1.

3.4 Jacobian of activation functions

In this subsection we elaborate on 𝐉𝐳[l]​(𝐟[l]​(𝐳[l]))subscript𝐉superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝑙superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝑙superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝑙\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}^{[l]}}\Big{(}\mathbf{f}^{[l]}\big{(}\mathbf{z}^{[l]}\big{)}\Big{)} where 𝐟[l]superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝑙\mathbf{f}^{[l]} is the l𝑙l-th activation layer whose corresponding input is 𝐳[l]superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝑙\mathbf{z}^{[l]} for l=1,…,L−1𝑙1…𝐿1l=1,\dots,L-1. When l≠L𝑙𝐿l\neq L, the vector 𝐟[l]​(𝐳[l])superscript𝐟delimited-[]𝑙superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝑙\mathbf{f}^{[l]}(\mathbf{z}^{[l]}) is typically obtained by applying a single univariate function f𝑓f to each element of 𝐳[l]superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝑙\mathbf{z}^{[l]}. The most common activation function used in DNNs is the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) function, defined as f​(x)=max⁡(0,x)𝑓𝑥0𝑥f(x)=\max(0,x) Fukushima (1969; 1975); Rumelhart et al. (1986a); Nair & Hinton (2010). Algebraically, this operation can be represented as

𝐟⊤​(𝐳)=[f​(z1)⋯f​(zd)]⊤superscript𝐟top𝐳superscriptmatrix𝑓subscript𝑧1⋯𝑓subscript𝑧𝑑top\mathbf{f}^{\top}\big{(}\mathbf{z}\big{)}=\begin{bmatrix}f(z_{1})&\cdots&f(z_{d})\end{bmatrix}^{\top}

where f𝑓f is the same univariate function applied to all elements of 𝐳𝐳\mathbf{z}, d𝑑d is the output size of the layer, and we have omitted superscripts for clarity. This special structure results in a diagonal matrix, i.e.,

𝐉𝐳​(𝐟​(𝐳))=diag​(f′​(z1),⋯,f′​(zd))subscript𝐉𝐳𝐟𝐳diagsuperscript𝑓′subscript𝑧1⋯superscript𝑓′subscript𝑧𝑑\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}}\big{(}\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{z})\big{)}=\text{diag}\Big{(}f^{\prime}(z_{1}),\cdots,f^{\prime}(z_{d})\Big{)}

where f′superscript𝑓′f^{\prime} is the derivative of a univariate function. For the special case of the ReLU function 𝐉𝐳​(𝐟​(𝐳))subscript𝐉𝐳𝐟𝐳\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}}\big{(}\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{z})\big{)} is a diagonal matrix of zeros and ones which are associated to the negative and positive elements of 𝐳𝐳\mathbf{z}. Multiplying such a matrix from the left to any matrix 𝐖𝐖\mathbf{W} results in removing the rows of 𝐖𝐖\mathbf{W} associated to zero elements in 𝐉𝐳​(𝐟​(𝐳))subscript𝐉𝐳𝐟𝐳\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}}\big{(}\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{z})\big{)} which greatly decreases the computation. The zero-th norm of the parameter vector can be minimized in sparse optimizations using these intuitions, as noted in Damadi & Shen (2022b).

Note that the derivations so far have only considered fully connected networks. However, for computer vision tasks, CNN models are utilized, which employ convolution operations instead of matrix multiplication in some layers. In the next subsection, we will demonstrate how a convolutional layer can be transformed into a fully connected network.

3.5 Convolution as matrix multiplication

A 2-D convolution operation is a mathematical operation that is used to extract features or patterns from a 2-dimensional input (matrix), such as an image. It works by applying a filter or kernel, which is also a matrix, to the input image LeCun et al. (1998). The filter is moved across the image, performing element-wise multiplications with the overlapping regions of the image and filter, and then summing the results. This process is repeated for every position of the filter on the image, resulting in a new matrix output, known as a feature map. Fig. 7 shows the process of a convolution operation where the 3x3 and 2x2 matrices represent the input and the filter respectively. As Fig. 7 illustrates the filter slides over the input image, one pixel at a time, and performs element-wise multiplications with the overlapping region of the image. The result of these multiplications is then summed, and the sum is stored in the corresponding location of the output feature map.

The size of the filter and the stride (the number of pixels the filter is moved each time) determine the size of the output feature map. Additionally, the filter can be applied multiple times with different filter parameters, to extract different features from the same input image.

Lemma 2.

The convolution operation between two matrices, 𝐗𝐗\mathbf{X} and 𝐊𝐊\mathbf{K}, can be represented as a matrix multiplication. Specifically, it can be represented as the product of a Toeplitz matrix (or diagonal-constant matrix) of 𝐊𝐊\mathbf{K} and the vector obtained from stacking the columns of the transpose of 𝐗𝐗\mathbf{X} in the order of the first one on top. Mathematically, this can be represented as:

𝐗∗𝐊=Teop​(𝐊)​Vec​(𝐗⊤)𝐗𝐊Teop𝐊Vecsuperscript𝐗top\mathbf{X}*\mathbf{K}=\text{Teop}(\mathbf{K})\text{Vec}(\mathbf{X}^{\top})

where 𝐗∈ℝmX×nX𝐗superscriptℝsubscript𝑚𝑋subscript𝑛𝑋\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{m_{X}\times n_{X}} and 𝐊∈ℝmK×nK𝐊superscriptℝsubscript𝑚𝐾subscript𝑛𝐾\mathbf{K}\in\mathbb{R}^{m_{K}\times n_{K}} and mX,nX,mX,nX∈ℕsubscript𝑚𝑋subscript𝑛𝑋subscript𝑚𝑋subscript𝑛𝑋ℕm_{X},n_{X},m_{X},n_{X}\in\mathbb{N}.

Remark 1.

The above representation allows for the convolution operation to be computed efficiently using matrix multiplication, which can be parallelized and accelerated on a GPU.

To clarify the above lemma, consider 𝐗∈ℝ3×3𝐗superscriptℝ33\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{3\times 3} and 𝐊∈ℝ2×2𝐊superscriptℝ22\mathbf{K}\in\mathbb{R}^{2\times 2} as the input and filter respectively. Then, one can verify the lemma by writing the following:

𝐗∗𝐊=[k1k20k3k400000k1k20k3k400000k1k20k3k400000k1k20k3k40]​[x1x2x3x4x5x6x7x8x9]𝐗𝐊matrixsubscript𝑘1subscript𝑘20subscript𝑘3subscript𝑘400000subscript𝑘1subscript𝑘20subscript𝑘3subscript𝑘400000subscript𝑘1subscript𝑘20subscript𝑘3subscript𝑘400000subscript𝑘1subscript𝑘20subscript𝑘3subscript𝑘40matrixsubscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3subscript𝑥4subscript𝑥5subscript𝑥6subscript𝑥7subscript𝑥8subscript𝑥9\mathbf{X}*\mathbf{K}=\begin{bmatrix}k_{1}&k_{2}&0&k_{3}&k_{4}&0&0&0&0\\ 0&k_{1}&k_{2}&0&k_{3}&k_{4}&0&0&0\\ 0&0&k_{1}&k_{2}&0&k_{3}&k_{4}&0&0\\ 0&0&0&k_{1}&k_{2}&0&k_{3}&k_{4}&0\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}x_{1}\\ x_{2}\\ x_{3}\\ x_{4}\\ x_{5}\\ x_{6}\\ x_{7}\\ x_{8}\\ x_{9}\end{bmatrix}
Lemma 3.

For an input 𝐗𝐗\mathbf{X} to a convolutional layer that has r𝑟r number of filters, each calculation 𝐗∗𝐊i+𝐁i𝐗subscript𝐊𝑖subscript𝐁𝑖\mathbf{X}*\mathbf{K}_{i}+\mathbf{B}_{i} is equivalent to (𝐖i)⊤​𝐱+𝐛isuperscriptsubscript𝐖𝑖top𝐱subscript𝐛𝑖(\mathbf{W}_{i})^{\top}\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}_{i} where 𝐊isubscript𝐊𝑖\mathbf{K}_{i} is the matrix of the i𝑖i-th filter, 𝐁isubscript𝐁𝑖\mathbf{B}_{i} is the matrix of associated bias for each filter, 𝐖i=(Teop​(𝐊i))⊤subscript𝐖𝑖superscriptTeopsubscript𝐊𝑖top\mathbf{W}_{i}=\Big{(}\text{Teop}(\mathbf{K}_{i})\Big{)}^{\top}, 𝐛i=Vec​(𝐁i)subscript𝐛𝑖Vecsubscript𝐁𝑖\mathbf{b}_{i}=\text{Vec}(\mathbf{B}_{i}) for i=1,…,r𝑖1…𝑟i=1,\dots,r, and 𝐱:=Vec​(𝐗⊤)assign𝐱Vecsuperscript𝐗top\mathbf{x}:=\text{Vec}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}).

According to the above lemma, we can convert a convolutional neural network to a typical fully connected one and find its gradient.

4 Conclusion

In this paper, we demonstrated the utilization of the Jacobian operator to simplify the gradient calculation process in DNNs. We presented a matrix multiplication-based algorithm that expresses the BP algorithm using Jacobian matrices and applied it to determine gradients for single, double, and three-layer networks. Our calculations offered insights into the gradients of loss functions in DNNs; for instance, the gradient of a single-layer network can serve as a model for the final layer of any DNN. We also expanded our findings to cover more intricate architectures such as LeNet-100-300-10 and demonstrated that the gradients of convolutional neural network layers can be transformed into linear layers. These results can aid research on compressing DNNs that utilize the full gradient, as noted in Damadi et al. (2022). Furthermore, they can benefit sparse optimization in both deterministic and stochastic settings, where the Iterative Hard Thresholding (IHT) algorithm uses the full gradient for a sparse solution in deterministic settings Damadi & Shen (2022b) and the mini-batch Stochastic IHT algorithm is employed in the stochastic context Damadi & Shen (2022a). We provided concise mathematical justifications to make the results clear and useful for people from different fields, even those without a deep understanding of the involved mathematics. This was particularly important when communicating complex technical concepts to non-experts as it allowed for a clear and accurate understanding of the results. Additionally, using mathematical notation allowed for precise and unambiguous statements of results, facilitating replication and further research in the field. As next steps, we intend to study the calculation of gradients for loss functions in various types of neural networks such as residual, recurrent, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Transformer networks. We will also explore the Jacobian of batch normalization to further our understanding of the method.

Appendix A Operators

Definition 1 (Jacobian matrix of a vector-valued function).

Let 𝐟:ℝn→ℝm:𝐟→superscriptℝ𝑛superscriptℝ𝑚\mathbf{f}:\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R}^{m} be a differentiable vector-valued function where 𝐟​(𝐱)=[f1​(𝐱)⋮fm​(𝐱)]𝐟𝐱matrixsubscript𝑓1𝐱⋮subscript𝑓𝑚𝐱\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})=\begin{bmatrix}f_{1}(\mathbf{x})\\ \vdots\\ f_{m}(\mathbf{x})\end{bmatrix} and 𝐱∈ℝn𝐱superscriptℝ𝑛\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}. This function takes a point 𝐱∈ℝn𝐱superscriptℝ𝑛\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n} as an input and produces 𝐟​(𝐱)∈ℝm𝐟𝐱superscriptℝ𝑚\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})\in\mathbb{R}^{m} as the output. The Jacobian matrix of 𝐟𝐟\mathbf{f} with respect to 𝐱𝐱\mathbf{x} is defined to be an m×n𝑚𝑛m\times n matrix denoted by 𝐉𝐱​𝐟​(𝐱)subscript𝐉𝐱𝐟𝐱\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) as the following:

𝐉𝐱​𝐟​(𝐱)=[∂f1​(𝐱)∂x1⋯∂f1​(𝐱)∂xn⋮⋱⋮∂fm​(𝐱)∂x1⋯∂fm​(𝐱)∂xn]=[(∇𝐱f1​(𝐱))⊤⋮(∇𝐱fm​(𝐱))⊤].subscript𝐉𝐱𝐟𝐱matrixsubscript𝑓1𝐱subscript𝑥1⋯subscript𝑓1𝐱subscript𝑥𝑛⋮⋱⋮subscript𝑓𝑚𝐱subscript𝑥1⋯subscript𝑓𝑚𝐱subscript𝑥𝑛matrixsuperscriptsubscript∇𝐱subscript𝑓1𝐱top⋮superscriptsubscript∇𝐱subscript𝑓𝑚𝐱top\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})=\begin{bmatrix}\frac{\partial f_{1}(\mathbf{x})}{\partial x_{1}}&\cdots&\frac{\partial f_{1}(\mathbf{x})}{\partial x_{n}}\\ \vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\ \frac{\partial f_{m}(\mathbf{x})}{\partial x_{1}}&\cdots&\frac{\partial f_{m}(\mathbf{x})}{\partial x_{n}}\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}\big{(}\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}f_{1}(\mathbf{x})\big{)}^{\top}\\ \vdots\\ \big{(}\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}f_{m}(\mathbf{x})\big{)}^{\top}\end{bmatrix}.
Definition 2 (Gradient of a scalar-valued function).

Let f:ℝ→ℝ:𝑓→ℝℝf:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} be a differentiable scalar-valued function. The gradient of ∇f:ℝn→ℝn:∇𝑓→superscriptℝ𝑛superscriptℝ𝑛\nabla f:\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R}^{n} at 𝐱∈ℝn𝐱superscriptℝ𝑛\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n} is defined as the following:

∇f​(𝐱)=[∂f​(𝐱)∂x1⋮∂f​(𝐱)∂xn].∇𝑓𝐱matrix𝑓𝐱subscript𝑥1⋮𝑓𝐱subscript𝑥𝑛\nabla f(\mathbf{x})=\begin{bmatrix}\frac{\partial f(\mathbf{x})}{\partial x_{1}}\\ \vdots\\ \frac{\partial f(\mathbf{x})}{\partial x_{n}}\end{bmatrix}.

Appendix B Activation functions

Definition 3 (Sigmoid function).

A sigmoid function σ:ℝ→[0,1]:𝜎→ℝ01\sigma:\mathbb{R}\to[0,1] is defined as σ​(x)=ex1+ex𝜎𝑥superscript𝑒𝑥1superscript𝑒𝑥\sigma(x)=\frac{e^{x}}{1+e^{x}}.

Definition 4 (Softmax function).

A softmax function 𝛔:ℝc→ℝc:𝛔→superscriptℝ𝑐superscriptℝ𝑐\bm{\sigma}:\mathbb{R}^{c}\to\mathbb{R}^{c} is defined for c≥3𝑐3c\geq 3 as the following:

𝝈​(𝐱)=[ex1∑j=1cexj⋮exc∑j=1cexj]∈ℝc.𝝈𝐱matrixsuperscript𝑒subscript𝑥1superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑐superscript𝑒subscript𝑥𝑗⋮superscript𝑒subscript𝑥𝑐superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑐superscript𝑒subscript𝑥𝑗superscriptℝ𝑐\bm{\sigma}(\mathbf{x})=\begin{bmatrix}\frac{e^{x_{1}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{c}e^{x_{j}}}\\ \vdots\\ \frac{e^{x_{c}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{c}e^{x_{j}}}\end{bmatrix}\in\mathbb{R}^{c}.

Appendix C Loss functions

Definition 5 (Binary Cross Entropy Loss).

Let y^∈[0,1]^𝑦01\hat{y}\in[0,1] be a predicted probability for a true label whose value is either zero or one, i.e., y∈{0,1}𝑦01y\in\{0,1\}. The Binary Cross Entropy (BCE) loss is defined as follows:

BCE​(y,y^)=−(y​log⁡(y^)+(1−y)​log⁡(1−y^)).BCE𝑦^𝑦𝑦^𝑦1𝑦1^𝑦\text{BCE}(y,\hat{y})=-\Big{(}y\log(\hat{y})+(1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\Big{)}.
Remark 2 (BCE loss).

The BCE loss is minimized when the predicted label is close to the true label. The BCE loss has a smooth and continuous gradient which makes it suitable for use with gradient-based optimization algorithms. It is also a convex function with respect to the variable y^^𝑦\hat{y}.

Definition 6 (Cross Entropy Loss).

Let 𝐲^∈(0,1)c∈ℝc^𝐲superscript01𝑐superscriptℝ𝑐\hat{\mathbf{y}}\in(0,1)^{c}\in\mathbb{R}^{c} (c≥3𝑐3c\geq 3) be a predicted probability for a true one-hot vector label 𝐲∈ℝc𝐲superscriptℝ𝑐\mathbf{y}\in\mathbb{R}^{c}, i.e., yj=1,yi=0formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝑗1subscript𝑦𝑖0y_{j}=1,y_{i}=0 for j≠i=1,…,cformulae-sequence𝑗𝑖1…𝑐j\neq i=1,\dots,c. The Cross Entropy (CE) loss is defined as follows:

CE​(y,y^)=−∑i=1cyi​log⁡(y^i).CE𝑦^𝑦superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑐subscript𝑦𝑖subscript^𝑦𝑖\text{CE}(y,\hat{y})=-\sum_{i=1}^{c}y_{i}\log(\hat{y}_{i}).
Remark 3.

Cross entropy function is commonly used in machine learning and information theory to measure the difference between two probability distributions. It is often used as a loss function to evaluate the performance of classification models.

In the context of machine learning, cross entropy is typically used to measure the difference between the predicted probability distribution (outputted by the model) and the true probability distribution (which represents the actual labels of the data). The cross entropy loss function is designed to penalize the model when it assigns low probabilities to the true labels, and to reward the model when it assigns high probabilities to the true labels.

Definition 7 (Squared Error Loss).

Let y^∈ℝ^𝑦ℝ\hat{y}\in\mathbb{R} be a predicted value for a true label whose value is y∈ℝ𝑦ℝy\in\mathbb{R}. The Square Error (SE) loss is defined as follows:

SE​(y,y^)=(y−y^)2.SE𝑦^𝑦superscript𝑦^𝑦2\text{SE}(y,\hat{y})=(y-\hat{y})^{2}.

Appendix D Gradient of the loss with respect to 𝐳[L]superscript𝐳delimited-[]𝐿\mathbf{z}^{[L]}

D.1 Sigmoid with BCE

Lemma 4 (Gradient of BCE loss).

Let 𝐱(i)superscript𝐱𝑖\bm{x}^{(i)} be an input to a one-layer network solving binary classification with y(i)∈{0,1}superscript𝑦𝑖01y^{(i)}\in\{0,1\} be its true label and y^(i)=σ​(𝐰⊤​𝐱(i)+b)superscript^𝑦𝑖𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝐱𝑖𝑏\hat{y}^{(i)}=\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right) is the predicted probability corresponding to the input for i=1,…,N𝑖1…𝑁i=1,\dots,N. The gradient of the BCE loss is given as follows:

∇𝜽BCE​(y(i),σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b))=−(y(i)−σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b))​[𝒙(i)1]subscript∇𝜽BCEsuperscript𝑦𝑖𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝒙𝑖𝑏superscript𝑦𝑖𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝒙𝑖𝑏matrixsuperscript𝒙𝑖1\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\text{BCE}\left(y^{(i)},\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)=-\left(y^{(i)}-\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)\begin{bmatrix}\bm{x}^{(i)}\\ 1\end{bmatrix}

for i=1,…,N𝑖1…𝑁i=1,\dots,N.

Proof.

We calculate the following for a fixed i∈{1,…,N}𝑖1…𝑁i\in\{1,\dots,N\}:

∇𝜽(BCE​(y(i),σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b)))subscript∇𝜽BCEsuperscript𝑦𝑖𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝒙𝑖𝑏\displaystyle\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\Bigg{(}\text{BCE}\left(y^{(i)},\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)\Bigg{)} =−∇𝜽(y(i)log(σ(𝐰⊤𝒙(i)+b))\displaystyle=-\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\Bigg{(}y^{(i)}\log\left(\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)
+(1−y(i))log(1−σ(𝐰⊤𝒙(i)+b))).\displaystyle+(1-y^{(i)})\log\left(1-\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)\Bigg{)}.

To calculate the above observe the following:

∇𝜽(σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b))=[𝒙(i)1]​σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b)​(1−σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b)).subscript∇𝜽𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝒙𝑖𝑏matrixsuperscript𝒙𝑖1𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝒙𝑖𝑏1𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝒙𝑖𝑏\displaystyle\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\left(\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)=\begin{bmatrix}\bm{x}^{(i)}\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\left(1-\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right).

Hence, we can write the following:

∇𝜽ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝜽))subscript∇𝜽ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝜽\displaystyle\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\ell\big{(}\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\bm{\theta})\big{)} =∇𝜽(BCE​(y(i),σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b)))absentsubscript∇𝜽BCEsuperscript𝑦𝑖𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝒙𝑖𝑏\displaystyle=\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\Bigg{(}\text{BCE}\left(y^{(i)},\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)\Bigg{)}
=−(y(i)σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b)​(1−σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b))σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b)[𝒙(i)1]\displaystyle=-\Bigg{(}y^{(i)}\frac{\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\left(1-\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)}{\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)}\begin{bmatrix}\bm{x}^{(i)}\\ 1\end{bmatrix}
−(1−y(i))σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b)​(1−σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b))1−σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b)[𝒙(i)1])\displaystyle-(1-y^{(i)})\frac{\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\left(1-\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)}{1-\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)}\begin{bmatrix}\bm{x}^{(i)}\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\Bigg{)}
=−y(i)​(1−σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b))​[𝒙(i)1]absentsuperscript𝑦𝑖1𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝒙𝑖𝑏matrixsuperscript𝒙𝑖1\displaystyle=-y^{(i)}\left(1-\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)\begin{bmatrix}\bm{x}^{(i)}\\ 1\end{bmatrix}
+(1−y(i))​σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b)​[𝒙(i)1]1superscript𝑦𝑖𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝒙𝑖𝑏matrixsuperscript𝒙𝑖1\displaystyle+(1-y^{(i)})\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\begin{bmatrix}\bm{x}^{(i)}\\ 1\end{bmatrix}
=−(y(i)−σ​(𝐰⊤​𝒙(i)+b))​[𝒙(i)1]absentsuperscript𝑦𝑖𝜎superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝒙𝑖𝑏matrixsuperscript𝒙𝑖1\displaystyle=-\left(y^{(i)}-\sigma\left(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bm{x}^{(i)}+b\right)\right)\begin{bmatrix}\bm{x}^{(i)}\\ 1\end{bmatrix}

∎

D.2 Softamx with CE

Lemma 5 (Gradient of CE loss w.r.t 𝐳𝐳\mathbf{z}).

Let 𝐲^∈(0,1)c∈ℝc^𝐲superscript01𝑐superscriptℝ𝑐\hat{\mathbf{y}}\in(0,1)^{c}\in\mathbb{R}^{c} (c≥3𝑐3c\geq 3) be a predicted probability such that 𝐲^=𝛔​(𝐳)^𝐲𝛔𝐳\hat{\mathbf{y}}=\bm{\sigma}(\mathbf{z}) where 𝛔𝛔\bm{\sigma} is a softmax function as defined in Def. 4 and 𝐳∈ℝn𝐳superscriptℝ𝑛\mathbf{z}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}. And, let 𝐲𝐲\mathbf{y} be a true one-hot vector label 𝐲∈ℝc𝐲superscriptℝ𝑐\mathbf{y}\in\mathbb{R}^{c}, i.e., yj=1,yi=0formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝑗1subscript𝑦𝑖0y_{j}=1,y_{i}=0 for j≠i=1,…,cformulae-sequence𝑗𝑖1…𝑐j\neq i=1,\dots,c. The gradient of the CE loss with respect to 𝐳𝐳\mathbf{z} is the following:

∇𝐳ℓ​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝐳))=∇𝐳CE​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝐳))=−(𝐲−𝐲^).subscript∇𝐳ℓ𝐲^𝐲𝐳subscript∇𝐳CE𝐲^𝐲𝐳𝐲^𝐲\nabla_{\mathbf{z}}\ell\left(\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\mathbf{z})\right)=\nabla_{\mathbf{z}}\text{CE}\left(\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\mathbf{z})\right)=-(\mathbf{y}-\hat{\mathbf{y}}).
Proof.

To calculate ∇𝐳CE​(𝐲,𝐲^​(𝐳))subscript∇𝐳CE𝐲^𝐲𝐳\nabla_{\mathbf{z}}\text{CE}\left(\mathbf{y},\hat{\mathbf{y}}(\mathbf{z})\right) one can rewrite it as ∇𝐳CE​(𝐲,𝝈​(𝐳))subscript∇𝐳CE𝐲𝝈𝐳\nabla_{\mathbf{z}}\text{CE}\left(\mathbf{y},\bm{\sigma}(\mathbf{z})\right). Then the loss can be expanded as follows:

CE​(𝐲,𝝈​(𝐳))=−∑i=1cyi​log⁡(ezi∑j=1cezj)CE𝐲𝝈𝐳superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑐subscript𝑦𝑖superscript𝑒subscript𝑧𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑐superscript𝑒subscript𝑧𝑗\text{CE}\left(\mathbf{y},\bm{\sigma}(\mathbf{z})\right)=-\sum_{i=1}^{c}y_{i}\log\left(\frac{e^{z_{i}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{c}e^{z_{j}}}\right)

each component can be calculated as the following:

∂∂zj​(−∑i=1cyi​(zi−log⁡(∑j=1cezj)))subscript𝑧𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑐subscript𝑦𝑖subscript𝑧𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑐superscript𝑒subscript𝑧𝑗\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{j}}\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{c}y_{i}\left(z_{i}-\log(\sum_{j=1}^{c}e^{z_{j}})\right)\right) =∂∂zj​(−∑i=1c(yi​zi)+∑i=1cyi​(log⁡(∑j=1cezj)))absentsubscript𝑧𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑐subscript𝑦𝑖subscript𝑧𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑐subscript𝑦𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑐superscript𝑒subscript𝑧𝑗\displaystyle=\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{j}}\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{c}(y_{i}z_{i})+\sum_{i=1}^{c}y_{i}\left(\log(\sum_{j=1}^{c}e^{z_{j}})\right)\right)
=−yj+∑i=1cyi​ezj∑j=1cezjabsentsubscript𝑦𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑐subscript𝑦𝑖superscript𝑒subscript𝑧𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑐superscript𝑒subscript𝑧𝑗\displaystyle=-y_{j}+\sum_{i=1}^{c}y_{i}\frac{e^{z_{j}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{c}e^{z_{j}}}
=−yj+∑i=1cyi​(𝝈​(𝐳))jabsentsubscript𝑦𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑐subscript𝑦𝑖subscript𝝈𝐳𝑗\displaystyle=-y_{j}+\sum_{i=1}^{c}y_{i}(\bm{\sigma}(\mathbf{z}))_{j}
=−yj+(𝝈​(𝐳))j​∑i=1cyiabsentsubscript𝑦𝑗subscript𝝈𝐳𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑐subscript𝑦𝑖\displaystyle=-y_{j}+(\bm{\sigma}(\mathbf{z}))_{j}\sum_{i=1}^{c}y_{i}
=−yj+(𝝈​(𝐳))jabsentsubscript𝑦𝑗subscript𝝈𝐳𝑗\displaystyle=-y_{j}+(\bm{\sigma}(\mathbf{z}))_{j}

where in the last equality we have use the fact that ∑i=1cyi=1superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑐subscript𝑦𝑖1\sum_{i=1}^{c}y_{i}=1. Since the above calculation is for the j𝑗j-th component and (𝐲^)j=(𝝈​(𝐳))jsubscript^𝐲𝑗subscript𝝈𝐳𝑗(\hat{\mathbf{y}})_{j}=(\bm{\sigma}(\mathbf{z}))_{j}, by calculating other components we get the desired result. ∎

D.3 Square Error

Lemma 6.

Let 𝐱(i)superscript𝐱𝑖\bm{x}^{(i)} be an input to a one-layer network with y(i)∈ℝsuperscript𝑦𝑖ℝy^{(i)}\in\mathbb{R} be the corresponding true value and

y^(i)=𝐰⊤​𝐱(i)+by^(i)​i​n​ℝsuperscript^𝑦𝑖superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝐱𝑖𝑏superscript^𝑦𝑖𝑖𝑛ℝ\hat{y}^{(i)}=\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{(i)}+b\quad\hat{y}^{(i)}in\mathbb{R}

be the corresponding prediction for i=1,…,N𝑖1…𝑁i=1,\dots,N. The gradient of SE loss is defined as follows:

∇𝜽SE​(y(i),𝐰⊤​𝐱(i)+b)subscript∇𝜽SEsuperscript𝑦𝑖superscript𝐰topsuperscript𝐱𝑖𝑏\displaystyle\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\text{SE}(y^{(i)},\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{(i)}+b) =∇𝜽(y(i)−(𝐰⊤𝐱(i)+b))2\displaystyle=\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}(y^{(i)}-(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{(i)}+b))^{2}
=−2​[𝐱(i)1]​(y(i)−y^(i))absent2matrixsuperscript𝐱𝑖1superscript𝑦𝑖superscript^𝑦𝑖\displaystyle=-2\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\\ 1\end{bmatrix}(y^{(i)}-\hat{y}^{(i)})

for i=1,…,N𝑖1…𝑁i=1,\dots,N.

Proof.

Let 𝜽:=[𝐰⊤​b]⊤assign𝜽superscriptdelimited-[]superscript𝐰top𝑏top\bm{\theta}:=[\mathbf{w}^{\top}b]^{\top}. Then,

∇𝜽(y(i)−y^(i))2=−2(y(i)−y^(i))∇𝜽y^(i)=−2[𝐱(i)1](y(i)−y^(i)).\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}(y^{(i)}-\hat{y}^{(i)})^{2}=-2(y^{(i)}-\hat{y}^{(i)})\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\hat{y}^{(i)}=-2\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\\ 1\end{bmatrix}(y^{(i)}-\hat{y}^{(i)}).

∎
